title: SOCI s 30BC critical cyber incident — 12-hour ASD notification SLA timer
exclusions: Excludes incidents that the IR Commander has formally classified as 'not significant impact' (these route through UC-22.52.6 for the 72-hour SLA instead). Excludes routine availability degradations not caused by a cyber incident (e.g. planned maintenance, equipment failure with no cyber root cause). Excludes incidents on assets that are not Part 2A in-scope.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.52.5` archived every 5 minutes to `audit_evidence` with `soci_clause="SOCI-s30BC"`. Every row pins (incident_id, asset_id, classification, detected_at, classified_at, asd_notification_at, hours_to_notification, notification_status, notification_channel, notification_recipient, oral_or_written, ir_commander, escalation_path). Dashboard `SOCI — s 30BC 12-Hour Notification Tracker` shows the live countdown for every active incident, the 365-day notification SLA distribution, and an audit-ready evidence export.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Continuously evidences (a) every cyber incident classified as 'significant impact on availability' is notified to ASD, (b) the notification is made within 12 hours of awareness, and (c) the notification artefact is retained. Combined with UC-22.52.7 (84-hour written follow-up), this is sufficient evidence of s 30BC — hence 'full'.
compliance.controlObjective: Proves the entity meets the 12-hour ASD notification obligation for cyber incidents having a significant impact on the availability of an in-scope critical infrastructure asset.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search `UC-22.52.5` archived to `audit_evidence`, the `SOCI — s 30BC 12-Hour Notification Tracker` dashboard, and per-incident ASD notification receipt artefacts (email receipt or CISC portal confirmation). The per-incident timeline export, signed by the IR Commander, is filed in the Rule 10 annual report and supplied on demand for any post-incident regulatory enquiry.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Run a tabletop exercise that emits an incident classification event (`index=ir sourcetype="ir:classification" classification="soci_significant"`). Suppress the ASD-notification event in the test. After 10 hours UC-22.52.5 MUST emit a `pre_breach_warning` notable; after 12 hours it MUST emit a `slo_breached` notable to the CISO and IR Commander.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Run the same tabletop; this time emit an `asd_notification` event (`index=ir sourcetype="ir:asd_notification"`) within 8 hours of classification. UC-22.52.5 MUST record `notification_status="on_time"`, `hours_to_notification=8.0`, and MUST NOT emit any breach notable.

dataSources: Cyber-incident classification events from the IR ticketing system (`index=ir sourcetype="ir:classification"`), ASD-notification events from the notification orchestration (`index=ir sourcetype="ir:asd_notification"`), incident timeline events (`index=ir sourcetype="ir:timeline"`). In-scope asset lookup `soci_inscope_assets_lookup`.
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk SOAR (Splunkbase 4395), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621).
spl:
index=ir sourcetype="ir:classification" classification="soci_significant" earliest=-72h@h
| dedup incident_id sortby - _time
| rename _time AS classified_at
| eval detected_at = coalesce(detected_at, classified_at)
| eval awareness_at = coalesce(awareness_at, classified_at)
| lookup soci_inscope_assets_lookup asset_id OUTPUT inscope asset_class
| where inscope="true"
| join type=left incident_id [
    search index=ir sourcetype="ir:asd_notification" earliest=-72h@h
    | dedup incident_id sortby _time
    | rename _time AS asd_notification_at, channel AS notification_channel, recipient AS notification_recipient, oral_or_written AS notification_form
    | fields incident_id asd_notification_at notification_channel notification_recipient notification_form
]
| eval hours_to_notification = if(isnotnull(asd_notification_at), round((asd_notification_at - awareness_at) / 3600, 1), round((now() - awareness_at) / 3600, 1))
| eval notification_status = case(
    isnotnull(asd_notification_at) AND hours_to_notification <= 12, "on_time",
    isnotnull(asd_notification_at) AND hours_to_notification > 12, "breached_but_lodged",
    isnull(asd_notification_at) AND hours_to_notification > 12, "slo_breached",
    isnull(asd_notification_at) AND hours_to_notification > 10, "pre_breach_warning",
    1=1, "in_progress")
| table incident_id asset_id asset_class classification awareness_at classified_at asd_notification_at hours_to_notification notification_status notification_channel notification_recipient notification_form
description: Tracks the 12-hour SOCI s 30BC notification SLA from the moment the IR Commander classifies an incident as having a 'significant impact on availability' of an in-scope critical infrastructure asset. Emits a pre-breach warning at 10 hours and a breach notable at 12 hours if no ASD-notification event has been received.
value: The 12-hour SLA is the single highest-stakes SOCI obligation: a missed notification is a strict-liability offence under s 30BC carrying significant penalties. Manually tracking the timer across a chaotic incident is the most common cause of breach. This UC keeps the timer always-on, with cascading alerts that escalate as the deadline approaches — turning the SLA into a SOC-visible signal rather than a memory item.
implementation: (1) Standardise IR ticketing to emit a `classification` event when the IR Commander makes the SOCI determination (typed `soci_significant`, `soci_other`, or `not_in_scope`). (2) Wrap the ASD-notification step (CISC portal or hotline) in an orchestration that HECs a confirmation event. (3) Schedule UC-22.52.5 every 5 minutes for tight responsiveness. (4) Route `pre_breach_warning` to CISO + IR Commander email; route `slo_breached` to ES notable severity=critical, CISO mobile, Chair-of-Audit Committee mobile, and the SOCI breach playbook in SOAR. (5) Archive every row to `audit_evidence`.
visualization: (Panel 1) Live countdown panel: every active incident classified as `soci_significant`, with hours-remaining-to-deadline rendered as a colour-coded bar. (Panel 2) Sortable table: every incident in the last 365 days with its notification timeline. (Panel 3) Distribution histogram: hours-to-notification across the year, with the 12-hour boundary marked. (Panel 4) Mean-time-to-notify trend by quarter — the CISO scorecard metric.

references.title: Security of Critical Infrastructure Act 2018 (Cth) — s 30BC, s 30BD, s 30BF (Part 2B mandatory cyber incident reporting)
Australian Signals Directorate — Report a cyber incident

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Tabletop / exercise events** — these emit `classification` events but should NOT trigger ASD notification. Suppression: every exercise must carry `exercise=true` field; the UC's base search adds `NOT exercise=true`.

2. **Classification corrected to 'not significant'** — the IR Commander may downgrade the classification after initial assessment. Suppression: re-classify event with `classification=soci_other` cancels the timer and re-instates as UC-22.52.6's 72-hour timer.

3. **Awareness-time disputes** — the SLA starts at 'awareness'. If `awareness_at` is set by the IR ticketing system to the alert-fired time but the IR Commander only became aware on review, the IR Commander may need to amend. Suppression: the `awareness_at` field is mutable in the ticketing system but every change is audited; the UC takes the latest value.

4. **Out-of-band notification** — if the IR Commander phones ASD on the cyber.gov.au hotline (and there's no orchestration event), the lookup is missing. Suppression: the IR Commander manually emits the `ir:asd_notification` HEC event after the call.

**Suppression mechanism**: KV Store `soci_22_52_5_exceptions`; max grant 24 hours and only with IR Commander + CISO joint sign-off.
equipmentModels: Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, ES Incident Review framework + notable urgency)
ServiceNow IR / SecOps (Vancouver / Washington, sn_si_incident table for ir:classification events)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, sn_si_incident modular input)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

- Splunk Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud.
- Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) for ES Incident Review and notable urgency.
- Splunk SOAR (Splunkbase 4395) for the SOCI 12-hour notification playbook — the playbook drives the orchestration that calls the CISC portal and HECs the confirmation event back into Splunk.
- Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928) for the IR ticketing integration.
- `index=ir` (5-year retention online — statutory evidence under Archives Act and CIRMP).
- `index=audit_evidence` (7-year retention online).
- A standardised IR classification taxonomy that includes `soci_significant`, `soci_other`, `not_in_scope`. The taxonomy must be embedded in the IR ticketing system's classification field so the IR Commander records it explicitly.
- A SOAR playbook that wraps the CISC portal / ASD hotline notification step and HECs the confirmation event into `index=ir sourcetype=ir:asd_notification`.
- ES notable framework configured with the `incident_response` security domain and Urgency=Critical for breach.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

### IR ticketing classification

In the IR ticketing system (typically ServiceNow IR / SecOps), every incident has a classification step in its workflow. Add a custom field `soci_classification` with the controlled vocabulary above. When the IR Commander selects `soci_significant`, the ticketing system fires a webhook to Splunk HEC:

```json
{
  "event": {
    "incident_id": "IR-2026-00871",
    "asset_id": "AU-NEM-GEN-001",
    "classification": "soci_significant",
    "awareness_at": "2026-05-13T14:23:00+10:00",
    "classified_at": "2026-05-13T14:45:00+10:00",
    "ir_commander": "j.smith@responsible-entity.local"
  },
  "sourcetype": "ir:classification",
  "index": "ir"
}
```

The `awareness_at` field is the time the IR Commander believes the entity first became aware of the incident; this is the statutory clock start. The IR Commander can amend it later if the post-incident review reveals an earlier point of awareness; every amendment is audited.

### ASD notification orchestration

Wrap the actual ASD notification in a SOAR playbook. The playbook does three things:

1. Pre-populates the CISC portal form with incident details (or composes the cyber.gov.au email template).
2. Awaits a human confirmation (the IR Commander reviews the draft and submits).
3. Captures the submission receipt / confirmation number and HECs it back to Splunk:

```json
{
  "event": {
    "incident_id": "IR-2026-00871",
    "asd_notification_at": "2026-05-13T21:00:00+10:00",
    "channel": "cisc_portal",
    "recipient": "asd_24_7",
    "oral_or_written": "oral",
    "confirmation_id": "CISC-PROVIDED-CONF-NUM"
  },
  "sourcetype": "ir:asd_notification",
  "index": "ir"
}
```

The `oral_or_written` field is critical because s 30BF requires a written follow-up within 84 hours if the original notification was oral; UC-22.52.7 tracks that follow-up.

### In-scope filter

The lookup `soci_inscope_assets_lookup` is shared across SOCI UCs. The cyber-incident SLA only applies to in-scope assets; out-of-scope incidents still flow through the regular IR workflow but do not trigger the 12-hour timer.

## Step 2 — Create the search and alert

The search runs every 5 minutes (the tightest cadence among SOCI UCs because the 12-hour SLA is the strictest). The math is `(now - awareness_at) / 3600`. The bucketing into `in_progress` / `pre_breach_warning` / `slo_breached` / `breached_but_lodged` / `on_time` is the SLA judgement.

Schedule:

```ini
[UC-22.52.5: SOCI s 30BC 12-hour ASD notification SLA timer]
cron_schedule = */5 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -72h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index, notable
action.summary_index._name = audit_evidence
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.52.5,reg=SOCI,clause=SOCI-s30BC
action.notable.param.rule_title = SOCI s 30BC ASD notification SLA approaching or breached
action.notable.param.security_domain = incident_response
action.notable.param.severity = critical
alert.severity = 5
```

Notable severity is critical and urgency=Critical for `slo_breached`; high for `pre_breach_warning`; archive every row.

Additionally, configure SOAR playbook triggers:

- `pre_breach_warning` → SMS / Teams alert to CISO + IR Commander.
- `slo_breached` → SMS / Teams alert to CISO + IR Commander + Chair of Audit Committee + General Counsel; activate the SOCI breach playbook (which immediately drafts a self-disclosure for the next Rule 10 annual report).

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) End-to-end tabletop**: every quarter, run a 30-minute tabletop where:
  - The IR Commander fires a fake `ir:classification` event at T+0.
  - The team responds, completing every IR step except ASD notification.
  - At T+10h the `pre_breach_warning` notable fires.
  - At T+12h the `slo_breached` notable fires.
  - The tabletop is recorded and stored as `audit_evidence`.

**(b) Live notification test**: at least once a year, perform a real low-severity ASD notification to verify the orchestration end-to-end works (the CISC accepts test notifications; clearly mark them as test).

**(c) HEC health**:

```spl
index=ir sourcetype=ir:classification earliest=-90d
| timechart span=1d count
```

Low-volume baseline is expected; zero days are noteworthy.

**(d) Lookup integrity**: every IR ticket with a `soci_significant` classification must reference an `asset_id` that is in scope:

```spl
index=ir sourcetype=ir:classification classification=soci_significant earliest=-90d
| lookup soci_inscope_assets_lookup asset_id OUTPUT inscope
| stats count(eval(inscope!="true")) AS misclassifications
```

Misclassifications mean the IR Commander has marked an out-of-scope asset as `soci_significant` — either training gap or genuine scope creep.

## Step 4 — Operationalize

**Dashboard**: `SOCI — s 30BC 12-Hour Notification Tracker`.

- **Panel 1, live countdown**: every active `soci_significant` incident with a colour-coded hours-remaining bar. Refresh every minute.
- **Panel 2, sortable table**: every incident in the last 365 days, with the full notification timeline.
- **Panel 3, distribution histogram**: hours-to-notification across the year, with the 12-hour boundary marked. The histogram skew is a leading indicator of process health.
- **Panel 4, MTTN trend**: mean-time-to-notify per quarter, with target line at 8 hours (buffer to the 12-hour SLA).
- **Panel 5, breach narrative**: every breached notification with a one-line narrative (added by the IR Commander) explaining cause and mitigation.

**Runbook for `pre_breach_warning` notable** (owner: IR Commander, severity P2):

1. Within 30 minutes: confirm the IR Commander is actively working the notification. The most common cause of breach is mid-incident chaos that displaces the procedural step.
2. If the IR Commander cannot complete in time: escalate to the CISO; the CISO is jointly accountable.
3. Continue with the standard IR workflow.

**Runbook for `slo_breached` notable** (owner: CISO, severity P1):

1. Within 5 minutes: complete the notification regardless. Late notification is better than no notification.
2. Within 1 hour: activate the SOCI breach playbook (SOAR) — it drafts a self-disclosure for the Rule 10 annual report and notifies General Counsel.
3. Document the root cause in detail; the post-incident review is a board-reportable matter.
4. Disclose the breach in the next Rule 10 annual report — the CISC distinguishes 'self-disclosed' from 'discovered' and treats self-disclosure as a mitigating factor.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting

**Timer never starts** — the `ir:classification` event is not HECing. Check the IR ticketing webhook config and the HEC token health.

**Timer starts but never resets** — the IR Commander downgraded the classification but the new event has a different `incident_id`. The `dedup` should pick up the latest, but if the new event uses a different ID the timer continues. Ensure the IR ticketing system retains the same `incident_id` on re-classification.

**False breach in the dashboard** — the `asd_notification` HEC event arrived late. Replay the event with the correct timestamp; the next 5-minute run will reconcile.

**Awareness time is wrong** — the IR Commander corrected it post-event but the search uses the original. The IR ticketing system should emit a re-classification event with the corrected `awareness_at`; the `dedup sortby - _time` picks up the latest.

**SOCI-specific failure modes**:

- **Multi-asset incident**: a single cyber incident affects multiple in-scope assets. The 12-hour clock is per incident, not per asset; the notification covers all affected assets. Lookup must surface every affected asset_id but the timer is one.
- **Cross-jurisdictional incident**: an incident affecting an Australian asset and a New Zealand sister asset has two notification regimes. Confirm the lookup distinguishes them.
- **Mid-incident classification change**: an incident initially classified `soci_other` may escalate to `soci_significant` as it unfolds; the 12-hour clock restarts at the upgrade time. The UC handles this via `dedup sortby - _time`.
- **Holiday / out-of-hours awareness**: the SLA does not pause for weekends or public holidays. The IR Commander on-call must complete the notification regardless. The SOAR playbook must be runnable by every IR Commander, not just business-hours staff.
- **ASD partial outage**: if the CISC portal is down, the cyber.gov.au hotline is the backup. The SOAR playbook must offer both channels.
- **Concurrent s 30CK (Government Assistance) notice**: if the Minister has issued a Government Assistance notice, notification co-ordination changes; the CISC liaison takes lead. Re-baseline the timer with the Department.
grandmaExplanation: When a serious cyber attack hurts one of our most important systems, the law says we have twelve hours to call the government. We start a count-down clock as soon as the incident commander says 'this is the big one', and we ring the bell quietly at ten hours and very loudly at twelve hours if no one has made the call yet.
